title: Shoulder Dystocia in 31-Year-Old G2P1 at 39+5 Weeks with Insulin-Treated Gestational Diabetes + Macrosomia (Estimated Fetal Weight 4400 g): Turtle Sign Recognition, HELPERR Mnemonic Maneuver Sequence with Team Activation in 60 Seconds, Brachial Plexus Injury Surveillance + Newborn Resuscitation per NRP, Maternal Postpartum Hemorrhage Risk, and Neonatal Hypoglycemia Management in Infant of Diabetic Mother
subtitle: SHOULDER DYSTOCIA = OBSTETRIC EMERGENCY recognized when normal traction fails to deliver anterior shoulder after delivery of fetal head; occurs in ~0.6-1.4% of all deliveries (HIGHER in macrosomia 4000-4500 g 9-14%; >4500 g 20-50%; GDM-IDM 2-4× baseline risk independent of size due to disproportionate shoulder + chest growth); CLASSIC TURTLE SIGN = fetal head retracts against perineum after delivery (head appears + retreats like turtle); RISK FACTORS = MACROSOMIA (most modifiable but suspect at any weight); GDM/IDM disproportionate shoulder + chest growth (most important risk factor); prior shoulder dystocia (10-15% recurrence); maternal obesity; post-term + prolonged 2nd stage; instrumental delivery (forceps/vacuum); advanced maternal age; precipitous delivery; prior macrosomic infant. HELPERR MNEMONIC for SHOULDER DYSTOCIA maneuvers (ACOG + AAP recommended sequence — to be completed within 60 sec each + ideally entire sequence within ~5 min): (H) CALL FOR HELP — ACTIVATE obstetric emergency team immediately (additional OB + nursery resuscitation team + anesthesia + charge RN + scribe + documentation); start timer; (E) EVALUATE for EPISIOTOMY — controversial; episiotomy does not relieve dystocia (bony obstruction not soft tissue) but may provide more room for maneuvers — selective use; (L) LEGS — MCROBERTS MANEUVER = sharp flexion of maternal hips to abdomen (2 assistants flex thighs to chest) — flattens lumbar lordosis + rotates symphysis cephalad + increases inlet diameter; resolves ~40% of shoulder dystocia alone; (P) PRESSURE — SUPRAPUBIC PRESSURE applied just above symphysis pubis (NOT fundal pressure — fundal worsens by impacting shoulder against bone) — performed by assistant; combined with McRoberts; resolves further ~30% of cases; (E) ENTER — INTERNAL MANEUVERS = (1) Rubin maneuver (push fetal anterior shoulder toward fetal chest); (2) Woods corkscrew (rotate fetal posterior shoulder 180°); (3) reverse Woods (rotate anterior shoulder 180° in opposite direction); rotation reduces shoulder diameter; (R) REMOVE posterior arm = sweep posterior fetal arm across chest + deliver → reduces shoulder-to-shoulder diameter; one of most effective maneuvers (success >85%); (R) ROLL = Gaskin maneuver / all-fours position = patient rotated to hands-and-knees position; gravity + pelvic rotation may relieve impaction; LAST-RESORT MANEUVERS if standard fail: deliberate CLAVICULAR FRACTURE (intentional fracture of fetal clavicle to reduce shoulder diameter); ZAVANELLI MANEUVER = reversal of cardinal movements + push fetal head back into uterus → emergent cesarean (rarely needed; complex); SYMPHYSIOTOMY (surgical separation of pubic symphysis — rarely used in US, more common in developing countries); ABDOMINAL RESCUE per Sancetta laparotomy + push baby back. AVOID FUNDAL PRESSURE (worsens — impacts shoulder + can cause uterine rupture); AVOID EXCESSIVE TRACTION on fetal head (causes brachial plexus injury); DOCUMENT precise times of head delivery + shoulder delivery + maneuvers + responses + team members + timing for risk management + neonatal assessment. POTENTIAL COMPLICATIONS: NEONATAL = brachial plexus injury (Erb palsy C5-C6; Klumpke palsy C8-T1; total brachial plexus injury) typically resolves but ~5-10% permanent; clavicle fracture (often intentional for shoulder relief); humeral fracture; hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy (delayed delivery → hypoxia); death rare. MATERNAL = perineal lacerations (3rd/4th degree more common); postpartum hemorrhage (uterine atony from prolonged labor + manipulation); symphyseal separation; femoral neuropathy from McRoberts; bladder injury. INFANT OF DIABETIC MOTHER (IDM) management: NEONATAL HYPOGLYCEMIA (glucose <40 mg/dL in newborn at risk) — most concerning + immediate post-delivery + within first 1-4 hr; maternal hyperglycemia → fetal hyperinsulinemia → reactive hypoglycemia after umbilical cord clamping; SCREEN q30 min × 2 + q1h × 4-6 + q3h × 24 hr per institutional protocol; TREAT with breastfeeding/formula/IV dextrose if symptomatic or persistently low; additional IDM risks = MACROSOMIA + birth trauma + RDS (delayed surfactant from hyperinsulinemia) + polycythemia (hyperglycemia → increased EPO + erythropoiesis) + hyperbilirubinemia + hypocalcemia (maternal hyperglycemia → fetal hyperparathyroidism suppression) + hypomagnesemia + cardiomyopathy (transient septal hypertrophy resolves) + caudal regression syndrome rare + congenital heart disease + neural tube defects (more in T1DM-treated; less in GDM if well-controlled); LEADERSHIP + DELEGATION in obstetric emergency = team activation + role assignment in 60 sec + closed-loop communication + scribe + delegation of maneuvers + neonatal team activation + clear escalation + post-event debriefing within 30 min + sentinel event analysis if neonatal injury

labs.v: 31 y/o female A. Martinez G2P1 (G2P1: one prior term spontaneous vaginal delivery 3 yr ago — healthy baby boy 3.4 kg; no complications) at 39+5 weeks gestation in active labor. PMH: Gestational Diabetes Mellitus (GDM) diagnosed at 26 weeks gestation via 1-hour 50g GLT (glucose loading test) positive → 3-hr OGTT (oral glucose tolerance test) confirmed GDM; initially diet-controlled + glycemic targets not met → INSULIN therapy initiated at 28 weeks (NPH basal + rapid-acting prandial — NovoLog 0.7 units/kg/day total daily dose adjusted by weeks; pump considered but PT preferred MDI); hyperthyroidism stable (on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily); GERD; no other chronic medical conditions. SOCIAL: married 5 years to spouse (35, accountant); 3-yr-old son at home with grandmother during delivery (close family + supportive); parents (grandparents) at hospital; works as teacher; insurance excellent. OBSTETRIC HX: G2P1 — first pregnancy (3 yr ago) uncomplicated + spontaneous vaginal delivery at 40+2 + 3.4 kg healthy infant + no complications. CURRENT PREGNANCY: planned + healthy preconception; 1st trimester unremarkable; 2nd trimester 26 wk GDM diagnosis + management; 3rd trimester growth concerning — fetal weight estimated 4100 g at 36 wk + 4400 g at 38+5 wk by growth scan; reassuring NSTs + BPPs; cervical exam stable until this week; ELECTIVE INDUCTION scheduled for 40+0 (tomorrow) due to: (1) macrosomia (>4500g at 40 wk projected; ACOG considers offering cesarean for EFW >5000g in non-diabetic + >4500g in GDM diabetic; this patient marginal at 4500g GDM); (2) GDM management; (3) shared decision-making with patient + family + OB. PATIENT presented to L&D at 1430 today at 39+5 wk in active labor — SPONTANEOUS labor preceded planned induction. ASSESSMENT on arrival: cervix 8 cm + 100% effaced + 0 station + SROM 30 min before arrival with clear fluid (no meconium) + contractions every 2-3 min × 60-90 sec moderate-strong; FHR 142 baseline + moderate variability + accelerations + Category I tracing reassuring; estimated fetal weight 4400 g per growth scan 1 wk ago (>90th percentile macrosomia); maternal glucose finger stick 165 (on insulin drip 1 unit/hr); IV LR + Foley + epidural in place + maternal hemodynamic monitoring + continuous CTG. PRECIPITOUS progression — cervix 8 → 10 cm + 100% + +1 station by 1500 (30 min from admission); pushing × 30 min at 1530 with progression to +2 station + rotation; FHR 132-138 baseline + variable decelerations + Category II tracing being monitored closely (consistent with cord compression in late labor + recovers between contractions). DELIVERY APPROACH: GDM + macrosomia + shared decision-making → vaginal delivery attempt with PREPARED team for shoulder dystocia + macrosomia + IDM neonatal considerations; obstetric attending Dr. Lopez + RN J. Wilson + scribe RN + nursery resuscitation team alerted + ready for IDM management.
labs.r: GDM + macrosomia + LARGE INFANT OF DIABETIC MOTHER (IDM) is HIGH RISK delivery requiring multidisciplinary team preparation. ELECTIVE CESAREAN for EFW >4500g in GDM per ACOG (sometimes; shared decision-making); this patient marginal + chose vaginal trial. RECOGNITION + preparation for shoulder dystocia + IDM neonatal complications essential.

labs.n: SHOULDER DYSTOCIA recognition + HELPERR maneuvers + team coordination
labs.v: SHOULDER DYSTOCIA = OBSTETRIC EMERGENCY when normal traction fails to deliver anterior shoulder after fetal head delivery; occurs in ~0.6-1.4% of all deliveries (HIGHER in macrosomia 4000-4500g = 9-14%; >4500g = 20-50%; GDM/IDM 2-4× baseline risk independent of size due to disproportionate shoulder/chest growth). CLASSIC TURTLE SIGN = fetal head retracts against perineum after delivery (head appears + retreats); shoulder impaction behind maternal symphysis pubis or sacral promontory. RISK FACTORS this patient — GDM/IDM (most important — disproportionate shoulder/chest growth from fetal hyperinsulinemia from maternal hyperglycemia); MACROSOMIA EFW 4400 g (>90th percentile); prior macrosomic infant (her first was 3.4 kg — within normal but trending); maternal obesity (BMI 31 currently — borderline obese); precipitous delivery (faster onset less time for rotation); advanced maternal age 31 (mild risk factor). HELPERR MNEMONIC for SHOULDER DYSTOCIA MANEUVERS per ACOG/AAP recommendation (sequence and time-limited each step ~30 sec to 1 min; entire sequence ideally within 5 min): (H) CALL FOR HELP — ACTIVATE obstetric emergency team immediately (additional OB + nursery resuscitation + anesthesia + charge RN + scribe + family liaison); start timer; (E) EVALUATE for EPISIOTOMY — controversial; episiotomy does not relieve dystocia (bony obstruction not soft tissue) but may provide more room for maneuvers — selective use; not routine but may help if needed; (L) LEGS — MCROBERTS MANEUVER = sharp flexion of maternal hips to abdomen (2 assistants flex thighs to chest with knees toward shoulders) — flattens lumbar lordosis + rotates symphysis cephalad + increases pelvic inlet diameter; resolves ~40% of shoulder dystocia alone; first-line maneuver; (P) PRESSURE — SUPRAPUBIC PRESSURE applied just above symphysis pubis (NOT fundal pressure — fundal pressure IS CONTRAINDICATED + WORSENS by impacting shoulder against bone + can cause uterine rupture) — performed by assistant using lateral + downward + inward force on anterior fetal shoulder; combined with McRoberts; resolves additional ~30% of cases when combined; (E) ENTER + INTERNAL MANEUVERS — (1) RUBIN MANEUVER = push fetal anterior shoulder toward fetal chest (reduces shoulder diameter); (2) WOODS CORKSCREW = rotate fetal posterior shoulder 180° (rotation reduces shoulder diameter); (3) reverse Woods = rotate anterior shoulder 180° in opposite direction; rotation reduces bisacromial diameter; (R) REMOVE POSTERIOR ARM = sweep fetal posterior arm across chest + deliver → reduces shoulder-to-shoulder diameter substantially; one of MOST EFFECTIVE maneuvers (success >85%); (R) ROLL = GASKIN MANEUVER / all-fours position = patient rotated to hands-and-knees position; gravity + pelvic rotation may relieve impaction; useful if anesthesia + position permits. LAST-RESORT MANEUVERS if standard fail: DELIBERATE CLAVICULAR FRACTURE (intentional fracture of fetal clavicle to reduce shoulder diameter; controlled by physician); ZAVANELLI MANEUVER = reversal of cardinal movements + push fetal head back into uterus → emergent cesarean (rarely needed; complex); SYMPHYSIOTOMY (surgical separation of pubic symphysis — rarely used in US, more common in developing countries with limited cesarean access); ABDOMINAL RESCUE per Sancetta = laparotomy + push baby back to permit cesarean. AVOID FUNDAL PRESSURE absolutely (worsens — impacts shoulder + can cause uterine rupture); AVOID EXCESSIVE TRACTION on fetal head (causes brachial plexus injury — Erb palsy C5-C6 most common). DOCUMENT precise times of head delivery + each maneuver + responses + team members + timing for risk management + neonatal assessment; institutional documentation often required + multiple state laws + JCAHO standards.
labs.r: SHOULDER DYSTOCIA = obstetric emergency requiring HELPERR mnemonic systematic maneuvers + team activation + closed-loop communication. AVOID FUNDAL PRESSURE + AVOID EXCESSIVE TRACTION (brachial plexus injury). DOCUMENT precisely. RN role: team activation + scribe + maneuver assistance + escalation + family communication.

labs.v: LEADERSHIP + DELEGATION in OBSTETRIC EMERGENCY = critical for outcomes; SHOULDER DYSTOCIA is time-sensitive + high-acuity + team-dependent + every second matters (fetal hypoxia + acidosis worsens with delay; reduced complication rates with rapid resolution). TEAM ACTIVATION within 30 SECONDS of recognition: (1) PRIMARY CALL — OB attending recognizes + declares emergency + activates team; (2) CHARGE RN — overhead page + phone + bed alarm for additional staff; (3) ROLE ASSIGNMENTS in 30-60 sec via CLOSED-LOOP COMMUNICATION: TEAM LEADER (OB attending — overall coordination + decision-making); SCRIBE (charge RN or designated — documents precise time of head delivery + each maneuver + time + responses + team members + neonatal assessment); McROBERTS POSITIONING (2 nurses — one at each leg sharply flexing maternal thighs to abdomen + maintaining position); SUPRAPUBIC PRESSURE (assistant — OB resident or senior nurse — positioned at suprapubic + applying downward + lateral + inward force on anterior fetal shoulder); INTERNAL MANEUVERS (OB attending or experienced OB resident — performs Rubin + Woods corkscrew + remove posterior arm + other); NEONATAL RESUSCITATION TEAM (RN nursery + RT + neonatal NP at warmer ready + anticipating brachial plexus injury + birth depression + hypoglycemia in IDM); ANESTHESIA (consider for emergent cesarean if Zavanelli needed); FAMILY LIAISON (charge RN coordinates with family in room); CLOSED-LOOP COMMUNICATION = state task + state name + acknowledge + report completion; SBAR handoff between team members + post-event; DELEGATION 5 RIGHTS (Right Task + Person + Circumstance + Communication + Supervision); RN scope = positioning + suprapubic pressure + medication administration + neonatal resuscitation (RN-NP team) + family communication + documentation + escalation; LPN/LVN scope = supportive role + supply transport + not primary maneuvers (specialty competency required); CNA/UAP = runner + supplies + family liaison support + not primary care; CHAIN OF COMMAND for delegation concerns (primary RN → charge RN → CNS → CNO → Medical Director); DOCUMENTATION must be precise + multidisciplinary input + scribe primary + chart timeline + neonatal outcomes + complications. POST-EVENT DEBRIEFING within 30 min after stabilization + formal review at 24-48 hr + system improvement opportunities + just culture + recognition of team performance + second victim phenomenon (RN trauma from emergency — EAP + peer support); INSTITUTIONAL learning + protocol refinement + simulation training + skills maintenance.
labs.r: LEADERSHIP + DELEGATION in obstetric emergency = team activation + role assignment within 30-60 sec + closed-loop communication + scribe + multidisciplinary + post-event debriefing + system learning. RN role: team coordination + scribe + skill execution + family + escalation + just culture.

labs.n: NEONATAL OUTCOMES + brachial plexus injury surveillance + birth trauma
labs.v: NEONATAL OUTCOMES after shoulder dystocia: (1) BRACHIAL PLEXUS INJURY (BPI) — most common neonatal complication; ~1-30% of shoulder dystocia (varies); occurs primarily from EXCESSIVE TRACTION on fetal head during dystocia maneuvers; types: ERB-DUCHENNE PALSY (C5-C6 most common 90% — arm extended + adducted + internally rotated + 'waiter's tip' position; loss of biceps + deltoid + rotator cuff function); KLUMPKE PALSY (C8-T1 — claw hand + Horner syndrome possible; rare); TOTAL BRACHIAL PLEXUS INJURY (C5-T1 — entire arm flaccid + severe); MAJORITY (~70-90%) resolve spontaneously within 6-12 months with physical therapy; PERSISTENT (~5-10%) require surgical intervention (nerve grafting + transfer + tendon transfer + reconstruction) by pediatric orthopedic + plastic surgeon at 3-6 months + intensive PT/OT lifelong; (2) CLAVICLE FRACTURE — often intentional in shoulder relief (controlled by physician); spontaneous fractures occur in shoulder dystocia + macrosomia; usually heals well + position arm in sling; pain management + monitor; (3) HUMERAL FRACTURE — less common; treat with positioning + cast if needed; (4) HYPOXIC-ISCHEMIC ENCEPHALOPATHY (HIE) — from delayed delivery + cord compression + fetal hypoxia; assess Apgar + arterial cord pH (acidosis pH <7.0 + base deficit ≥12 = severe HIE risk + therapeutic hypothermia consideration); neonatal team monitoring + escalation; (5) FETAL DEATH — rare but possible with severe + prolonged dystocia; (6) OTHER birth trauma — cephalohematoma + caput succedaneum + facial nerve injury. ASSESSMENT for brachial plexus injury at delivery + nursery: arm position + symmetry + range of motion + reflexes (moro + grip + asymmetric tonic neck + sucking); pediatric ortho consult if concerns; PT/OT evaluation early. THIS PATIENT newborn: APGAR 6/9 (recovered well); weight 4350 g; right arm extended + diminished tone + limited movement + flexed elbow on right + early signs of ERB PALSY C5-C6 (anterior shoulder traction from dystocia); moro reflex weakened on right; no obvious clavicle fracture on palpation; pediatric ortho consult activated; PT/OT consultation; immobilize arm in elbow flexion across chest (no traction or pulling); positioning to support natural posture; expected partial spontaneous recovery + ongoing surveillance.
labs.r: NEONATAL OUTCOMES after shoulder dystocia primarily brachial plexus injury (Erb palsy C5-C6 most common; majority resolve; some require surgery); clavicle/humeral fracture; HIE rare with prolonged dystocia; assessment + pediatric ortho consult + PT/OT + immobilization + monitoring + family education. RN role: assessment + family education + multidisciplinary referral.

labs.n: MATERNAL COMPLICATIONS + postpartum hemorrhage risk + lacerations
labs.v: MATERNAL COMPLICATIONS after shoulder dystocia: (1) POSTPARTUM HEMORRHAGE (PPH) — ELEVATED RISK from prolonged labor + manipulation + macrosomia (over-distended uterus → atony) + manual maneuvers (uterine atony + lacerations); 4 Ts of PPH = TONE (uterine atony — most common cause; treat with uterotonics + massage + bimanual compression + tamponade balloon + IR + hysterectomy); TRAUMA (lacerations + uterine inversion + uterine rupture); TISSUE (retained products of conception); THROMBIN (coagulopathy + DIC); UTEROTONICS = oxytocin first-line + methylergonovine 0.2 mg IM (CONTRAINDICATED in HTN/preeclampsia) + carboprost 250 mcg IM q15 min × 8 (CONTRAINDICATED in asthma) + misoprostol 600-1000 mcg PR/PO/buccal; ESCALATION = uterine massage + bimanual compression + tamponade balloon (Bakri) + IR embolization + hysterectomy ladder; MASSIVE TRANSFUSION PROTOCOL if shock; (2) PERINEAL/VAGINAL LACERATIONS — 3rd/4th degree more common from manipulation + macrosomia; sutured by OB; pain management + ice + sitz baths + monitoring; (3) SYMPHYSEAL SEPARATION — uncommon; treated conservatively or surgically depending on severity + symptoms; (4) FEMORAL NEUROPATHY from McRoberts maneuver hyperflexion — uncommon; usually resolves; (5) BLADDER INJURY — uncommon; assess voiding + UA + ultrasound if concerns; (6) PSYCHOLOGICAL TRAUMA — birth experience + emergent intervention + concern for baby + may require counseling + support + EAP for staff + family. INFANT OF DIABETIC MOTHER (IDM) maternal considerations: continued insulin management postpartum (typically rapid decrease in insulin requirements; some women have transient GDM only + postpartum diabetes screen at 6-12 weeks + lifelong screening); breastfeeding strongly encouraged (improves maternal glucose + neonatal outcomes); contraception planning + future pregnancy planning + preconception counseling for next pregnancy (better GDM management + earlier OGTT + insulin pump consideration). FAMILY-CENTERED CARE + emotional support + chaplain + social worker + birth experience processing + bonding + breastfeeding + family integration. THIS PATIENT postpartum: 2nd degree perineal laceration + extensive vaginal mucosal laceration repaired; EBL ~600 mL (above 500 threshold for vaginal PPH; manageable); uterotonics responsive; uterine tone improving with massage + oxytocin; type & crossmatch pending; 2nd IV access in place; vital signs stable; emotional + family support throughout; spouse at bedside; postpartum vigilance × 24 hr + breastfeeding initiation.
labs.r: MATERNAL COMPLICATIONS after shoulder dystocia primarily PPH risk + lacerations + symphyseal separation + femoral neuropathy + psychological trauma; active management of 3rd stage + uterotonics + monitoring + laceration repair + emotional support. RN role: PPH surveillance + medication administration + family + psychological support.

labs.n: IDM NEONATAL HYPOGLYCEMIA + comprehensive newborn care
labs.v: INFANT OF DIABETIC MOTHER (IDM) RISKS + management: (1) NEONATAL HYPOGLYCEMIA — most concerning + immediate; pathophysiology = maternal hyperglycemia → fetal hyperinsulinemia (fetal pancreas hyperplasia to compensate) → at delivery + umbilical cord clamping the placental glucose source removed but fetal/newborn insulin levels remain elevated → REACTIVE HYPOGLYCEMIA; THRESHOLD varies by institutional protocol — typically <40 mg/dL in IDM at risk; SCREENING per protocol = glucose finger stick at 30 min + 1 hr + 2 hr + then q1h × 4-6 hr + q3h × 24 hr; SYMPTOMATIC vs asymptomatic — symptomatic (lethargy + jitteriness + apnea + seizure + poor feeding + cyanosis + temperature instability) warrants immediate treatment + IV dextrose; ASYMPTOMATIC hypoglycemia may be addressed with EARLY BREASTFEEDING + skin-to-skin + supplemental formula if breastfeeding unsuccessful + REPEAT screening; PERSISTENT or symptomatic = IV DEXTROSE 10% (D10) 2 mL/kg bolus then continuous infusion 60-80 mL/kg/day; THIS PATIENT newborn glucose 32 at 30 min = treat with breastfeeding/formula + repeat; (2) MACROSOMIA + birth trauma — addressed; assess for additional injuries; (3) RESPIRATORY DISTRESS SYNDROME (RDS) — delayed surfactant production from fetal hyperinsulinemia; may need supplemental O2 + CPAP + surfactant in late preterm IDM; (4) POLYCYTHEMIA — hyperglycemia → increased EPO + erythropoiesis → polycythemia; may cause hyperviscosity + jaundice + thrombosis; partial exchange transfusion if severe; (5) HYPERBILIRUBINEMIA — RBC breakdown + bruising from birth trauma + immature hepatic conjugation; phototherapy + monitoring + serial bilirubin levels; (6) HYPOCALCEMIA — maternal hyperglycemia suppresses fetal parathyroid → relative hypocalcemia at birth; treat with calcium gluconate; (7) HYPOMAGNESEMIA — similar mechanism; (8) CARDIOMYOPATHY — transient septal hypertrophy (hyperinsulinemia stimulates myocardial cells); usually resolves over months without treatment; echocardiography if signs; (9) CONGENITAL HEART DISEASE + NEURAL TUBE DEFECTS — increased risk in T1DM-treated (3-5× general population); less in GDM if well-controlled (~1.5×); evaluation; (10) CAUDAL REGRESSION SYNDROME — rare but unique to IDM. NRP (Neonatal Resuscitation Program) framework: (1) WARM + DRY + STIMULATE; (2) ASSESS HR + RESPIRATIONS; (3) PROVIDE PPV (positive-pressure ventilation) if needed (T-piece + room air initially per NRP 2020); (4) CHEST COMPRESSIONS if HR <60 with PPV; (5) EPINEPHRINE 0.1-0.3 mL/kg of 1:10,000 IV if HR <60 after PPV + compressions; (6) AIRWAY — intubation + suction; (7) RESPIRATIONS + ventilation; (8) ASSESSMENT + cardiopulmonary stability; algorithm by 1 min + 5 min + 10 min APGAR; team competency essential.
labs.r: IDM NEONATAL COMPLICATIONS = hypoglycemia (most immediate) + macrosomia + RDS + polycythemia + hyperbilirubinemia + hypocalcemia + hypomagnesemia + cardiomyopathy + congenital + neural tube. Standard monitoring + treatment per protocol. NRP framework for resuscitation. RN role: assessment + monitoring + treatment + family education + multidisciplinary.

labs.v: PERTINENT NEGATIVES: NO uterine rupture (intact fundal palpation + appropriate bleeding pattern + no hemodynamic collapse); NO neonatal HIE on initial assessment (APGAR 6/9 recovered; cord pH pending but vitality reassuring); NO neonatal death; NO MATERNAL CARDIOPULMONARY collapse (vital signs stable throughout); NO amniotic fluid embolism (no respiratory distress + no shock + no DIC currently — would be considered if maternal sudden deterioration); NO meconium-stained fluid (clear at SROM); NO acute infection signs (afebrile + cultures pending if concerns); NO eclampsia (no seizure + BP stable); NO arrhythmia or cardiac event; NO acute mental health crisis; allergies known + tolerated medications; insurance + multidisciplinary team available + experienced; level III facility with NICU + pediatric ortho + comprehensive support; family supportive + appropriate + receptive; PROTECTIVE FACTORS: SECOND PREGNANCY (parous → typically faster + more efficient labor + lower risk of complications than primigravid); insulin-treated GDM with EFFORT to control glucose (HbA1c trending well + insulin adherence + intensive monitoring); ANTEPARTUM PREPARATION + multidisciplinary team aware of macrosomia + IDM risks + ready for delivery; EARLY RECOGNITION of shoulder dystocia within 60 sec + immediate HELPERR maneuvers + skilled team available; RAPID RESOLUTION with combined McRoberts + suprapubic + Rubin + posterior arm delivery within ~3 minutes (within safe time window); NEONATAL RESUSCITATION TEAM ready + experienced; FAMILY supportive + extended family available; INSURANCE excellent + multidisciplinary outpatient care accessible; PATIENT motivated + receptive + cooperative; LIFELONG diabetes screening + future pregnancy planning + preconception counseling addressable; CULTURAL + spiritual support available + integrated.
labs.r: Despite serious obstetric emergency, multiple PROTECTIVE FACTORS supported good outcome: parity + GDM control + antepartum preparation + early recognition + immediate skilled response + experienced team + multidisciplinary support + family + insurance + cultural sensitivity. Standard expected positive trajectory with comprehensive intervention.

nursesNote: 1545 — SHOULDER DYSTOCIA RECOGNIZED in patient A. Martinez (31 y/o G2P1 at 39+5 weeks with insulin-treated GDM + macrosomia EFW 4400 g) by OB attending Dr. Lopez within 15 seconds of fetal head delivery when external rotation failed + TURTLE SIGN visible (head retracts against perineum). HISTORY: G2P1 (one prior healthy term vaginal delivery 3 yr ago with 3.4 kg infant); GDM diagnosed 26 wk + insulin therapy initiated 28 wk + on insulin drip 1 unit/hr during labor; elective induction scheduled for 40+0 tomorrow due to macrosomia + GDM but SPONTANEOUS labor preceded; arrived L&D at 1430 in active labor (cervix 8 cm + 100% + 0 station + SROM 30 min before arrival with clear fluid); precipitous progression to 10 cm by 1500 + pushing × 30 min from 1530; epidural in place; FHR Category I throughout most of labor + Category II tracing with variable decelerations in late 2nd stage being monitored closely; multidisciplinary team aware of macrosomia + IDM risks + ready for delivery. FETAL HEAD DELIVERED at 1545 BUT external rotation does NOT occur normally + head appears to retract against perineum = TURTLE SIGN. OB attending Dr. Lopez immediately recognizes shoulder dystocia at 1545:15 + calls 'SHOULDER DYSTOCIA, GET HELP, START TIMER'. OBSTETRIC EMERGENCY TEAM ACTIVATED within 30 seconds via overhead page + phone + bed alarm by charge RN. ROLE ASSIGNMENTS in 30 seconds via closed-loop communication: TEAM LEADER = OB attending Dr. Lopez (overall coordination + decision-making + execution of internal maneuvers); SCRIBE = charge RN C. Martinez (documents precise time of head delivery + each maneuver + responses + team members + neonatal assessment on standardized shoulder dystocia documentation form); MCROBERTS POSITIONING = RN J. Wilson + RN R. Patel (each at one of maternal legs sharply flexing maternal thighs to abdomen with knees toward shoulders + maintaining position throughout maneuvers); SUPRAPUBIC PRESSURE = OB resident Dr. Park (positioned suprapubic above symphysis pubis + applying downward + lateral + inward force on anterior fetal shoulder); INTERNAL MANEUVERS = OB attending Dr. Lopez (Rubin + remove posterior arm); NEONATAL RESUSCITATION TEAM at warmer ready = RN nursery + RT + neonatal NP (anticipating brachial plexus injury + birth depression + hypoglycemia in IDM); FAMILY LIAISON = charge RN coordinates communication with spouse who is at bedside (transparent + compassionate updates). HELPERR MANEUVERS executed in sequence: (H) HELP — team activated within 30 sec ✓; (E) EVALUATE EPISIOTOMY — not done initially (selective; bony obstruction not soft tissue); (L) LEGS — McROBERTS MANEUVER initiated at 1545:30 — RN J. Wilson + RN R. Patel sharply flex maternal hips to abdomen + hold position; goal = flatten lumbar lordosis + rotate symphysis cephalad + increase pelvic inlet diameter; resolves ~40% alone; (P) PRESSURE — SUPRAPUBIC PRESSURE applied by OB resident Dr. Park above symphysis pubis + downward + outward + inward force on anterior fetal shoulder; AVOID FUNDAL PRESSURE (contraindicated — worsens by impacting shoulder against bone + can cause uterine rupture); combined McRoberts + suprapubic pressure × 30 sec at 1546 — UNSUCCESSFUL anterior shoulder delivery; attempt 2 at 1546:30 — still unsuccessful; (E) ENTER + (R) REMOVE — OB attending decides internal maneuvers; RUBIN MANEUVER at 1546:45 (push fetal anterior shoulder toward fetal chest reducing shoulder diameter) — partial rotation; REMOVE POSTERIOR ARM at 1547 (OB sweeps fetal posterior arm across chest + delivers) — reduces shoulder-to-shoulder diameter substantially; SHOULDER DYSTOCIA RESOLVED at 1547:15 + body delivered immediately; TOTAL HEAD-TO-BODY INTERVAL = 2 minutes from head delivery to body delivery (within safe time window but indicates true dystocia). Avoided FUNDAL PRESSURE (contraindicated). Avoided EXCESSIVE TRACTION on fetal head (brachial plexus injury risk). Documentation precise. NEWBORN delivered at 1547: APGAR 6/9 (1 min: HR 130 + RR irregular + slight tone + cry weak + grimace + acrocyanotic = 6; 5 min: HR 140 + RR 30 + cry + tone improving + active flexion + acrocyanotic + crying with stimulation = 9 — RECOVERED well); weight 4350 g (LARGE for gestational age — confirms macrosomia); length 53 cm + head circumference 36 cm; nursery resuscitation team handed off + WARM + DRY + STIMULATE + assessment in nursery + thermoregulation + glucose monitoring per IDM protocol + brachial plexus injury assessment + pediatric ortho consult activated. INITIAL BPI ASSESSMENT (right arm extended at side + diminished tone + LIMITED movement on right + flexed elbow on right = early signs of ERB PALSY C5-C6 from anterior shoulder traction during dystocia maneuvers; left arm normal; moro reflex weakened on right; grip reflex bilateral; bones intact on rapid palpation — no obvious clavicle fracture); EXPECTED partial spontaneous recovery ~70-90% by 6-12 months with PT/OT; pediatric ortho consult activated for evaluation + plan + family education; arm immobilized in flexion across chest (no pulling or traction). MATERNAL POSTPARTUM 1550: placenta delivered intact + complete at 1550 with mild gentle traction; bleeding initially moderate then becoming heavier; UTEROTONICS — oxytocin 10 units IM thigh + 30 units in 1L LR IV infusion + fundal massage initiated; methylergonovine 0.2 mg IM contraindicated (NOT given — patient HTN history); carboprost 250 mcg IM available if needed (asthma not present; can use); misoprostol 800 mcg PR available; fundal tone soft initially → vigorous massage + oxytocin → tone improves; bleeding pattern monitored; PERINEAL/VAGINAL LACERATIONS — 2nd degree perineal laceration + extensive vaginal mucosal laceration repaired by OB; estimated blood loss so far ~600 mL (above 500 mL threshold for vaginal PPH but manageable); 2nd IV access placed + type & crossmatch pending; uterotonics responsive + bleeding controlled; fluid maintenance; bladder voiding assessed; vital signs stable; symphyseal separation assessed — no acute concerns + ROM OK + no severe pain; femoral neuropathy from McRoberts assessed — bilateral lower extremity sensation + motor intact; FAMILY communication — spouse at bedside + extended family in waiting room + chaplain offered + accepted (family Catholic — coordinated with hospital chaplain); social worker assessment + emotional support + understanding of events. NURSERY 1600 (15 min post-delivery): newborn glucose finger stick at 30 min = 32 mg/dL = NEONATAL HYPOGLYCEMIA (<40 in IDM at risk per institutional protocol; symptomatic vs asymptomatic — baby active + crying + good color + nursing attempt being offered + mother encouraged to breastfeed early); INTERVENTION = early breastfeeding + skin-to-skin + supplemental formula if breastfeeding unsuccessful + repeat glucose in 30 min; IV dextrose 10% (D10) 2 mL/kg bolus + continuous infusion 60-80 mL/kg/day if symptomatic or persistent hypoglycemia despite feeding; nursery RN frequent monitoring + thermoregulation + glucose surveillance per protocol (q30 min × 2 then q1h × 4-6 then q3h × 24 hr); brachial plexus injury ongoing assessment + pediatric ortho consult ongoing + PT/OT consultation + arm positioning + immobilization + family education. SHOULDER DYSTOCIA EMERGENCY PROTOCOL (executed):

HELPERR MNEMONIC executed per ACOG/AAP protocol:
H: Help — team activated
E: Evaluate episiotomy — not done initially (selective)
L: Legs (McRoberts) — sharp flexion of maternal hips to abdomen by 2 nurses
P: Pressure (Suprapubic) — applied above symphysis pubis + downward + inward force (NOT fundal pressure — CONTRAINDICATED)
E: Enter (Internal maneuvers) — Rubin maneuver (push anterior shoulder toward chest)

Last-resort maneuvers ready (clavicular fracture + Zavanelli + symphysiotomy + abdominal rescue) — not needed

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters at shoulder dystocia recognition?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are obstetric emergency + significant cues requiring nursing attention.

opts.text: SHOULDER DYSTOCIA EMERGENCY recognized — TURTLE SIGN (fetal head retracts against perineum + external rotation fails to occur normally) + anterior shoulder NOT delivering with normal gentle downward traction; OB attending Dr. Lopez immediately recognizes + calls 'shoulder dystocia, get help, start timer' at 1545:15 within 15 sec of head delivery
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — recognition of shoulder dystocia. RAPID recognition is essential — each second of delay increases hypoxia + brachial plexus injury risk. RN role: recognize + activate team + scribe.

opts.text: TEAM ACTIVATION within 30 seconds via overhead page + phone + bed alarm; ROLE ASSIGNMENTS in 30 seconds via closed-loop communication: TEAM LEADER (OB attending) + SCRIBE (charge RN documenting precise times) + McROBERTS (2 nurses each at one leg) + SUPRAPUBIC PRESSURE (OB resident) + INTERNAL MANEUVERS (OB attending) + NEONATAL RESUSCITATION TEAM (RN + RT + NNP at warmer) + FAMILY LIAISON (charge RN)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — team activation + role assignment within 60 sec via closed-loop communication. Time-sensitive emergency. RN role: facilitate + execute role + scribe + escalate.

opts.text: HELPERR MNEMONIC maneuvers executed in sequence per ACOG/AAP: (H) HELP team activated; (L) LEGS = McROBERTS (sharp flexion of maternal hips); (P) PRESSURE = SUPRAPUBIC (NOT fundal — contraindicated); (E) ENTER = internal maneuvers (Rubin); (R) REMOVE posterior arm = sweep across chest + deliver; combined McRoberts + suprapubic + Rubin + posterior arm RESOLVED shoulder dystocia at 1547:15 within ~2 min head-to-body interval
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — HELPERR mnemonic systematic maneuver sequence. Standard ACOG/AAP recommendation. Foundation for shoulder dystocia management. RN role: assist + execute + scribe + escalate.

opts.text: AVOID FUNDAL PRESSURE (CONTRAINDICATED — worsens by impacting shoulder against bone + can cause uterine rupture); AVOID EXCESSIVE TRACTION on fetal head (causes brachial plexus injury — Erb palsy C5-C6 most common); APPROPRIATE force only + standard maneuvers
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — contraindications. Fundal pressure WORSENS + can cause uterine rupture. Excessive traction causes brachial plexus injury. Standard practice. RN role: prevent contraindicated actions.

opts.text: NEONATAL OUTCOMES + brachial plexus injury surveillance: newborn weight 4350 g LGA + APGAR 6/9 (recovered well); right arm extended + diminished tone + limited movement + flexed elbow on right = ERB PALSY C5-C6 likely from anterior shoulder traction; pediatric ortho consult + PT/OT early intervention; immobilize arm in flexion across chest (NO pulling or traction); expected partial spontaneous recovery 70-90% by 6-12 months; surgical intervention at 3-6 months if persistent
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — neonatal brachial plexus injury surveillance + management. Most common neonatal complication of shoulder dystocia. RN role: assessment + family education + pediatric ortho + PT/OT + ongoing care.

opts.text: MATERNAL POSTPARTUM MANAGEMENT: oxytocin 10 IM + 30 in 1L LR IV + fundal massage; methylergonovine NOT given (HTN history); carboprost available; misoprostol available; bimanual compression + tamponade balloon + IR + hysterectomy ladder if persistent; EBL 600 mL (above PPH threshold but manageable); 2nd degree perineal + extensive vaginal mucosal lacerations repaired; vital signs stable; postpartum vigilance × 24 hr
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — maternal postpartum management + PPH risk after shoulder dystocia. Active management 3rd stage + uterotonics + monitoring + escalation ladder + laceration repair. RN role: vigilant monitoring + medication + escalation.

opts.text: IDM NEONATAL MANAGEMENT — newborn glucose 32 at 30 min = NEONATAL HYPOGLYCEMIA (<40 in IDM at risk); early breastfeeding + skin-to-skin + supplemental formula + repeat glucose in 30 min + IV dextrose if symptomatic/persistent; q30 min × 2 then q1h × 4-6 hr screening per IDM protocol; RDS + polycythemia + hyperbilirubinemia + hypocalcemia + hypomagnesemia + cardiomyopathy + congenital screening; thermoregulation
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — IDM neonatal management with hypoglycemia central concern. Standard IDM protocol. RN role: assessment + monitoring + intervention + multidisciplinary + family education.

opts.text: LEADERSHIP + DELEGATION in obstetric emergency: TEAM ACTIVATION within 30 sec + ROLE ASSIGNMENTS in 30-60 sec via closed-loop communication + scribe + multidisciplinary; CHAIN OF COMMAND (primary RN → charge RN → CNS → CNO → Medical Director); RN scope = positioning + suprapubic pressure + medication + neonatal resuscitation + family communication + documentation; LPN/CNA scope = supportive role + supply transport; POST-EVENT DEBRIEFING within 30 min + formal review at 24-48 hr + just culture + EAP + peer support + second victim phenomenon
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — leadership + delegation + team coordination + post-event response. Standard obstetric emergency practice. RN role: facilitate + execute + escalate + debriefing + just culture.

opts.text: FAMILY + multidisciplinary support: spouse at bedside + extended family in waiting room + chaplain (family Catholic — coordinated with hospital chaplain) + social worker + pediatric ortho + neonatology + lactation; transparent + compassionate post-event family meeting; emotional support throughout + cultural sensitivity
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — family-centered + multidisciplinary + emotional support. Foundation for trust + healing. RN role: facilitate + advocate + emotional support.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — document and proceed
opts.rat: Allergy documentation. No contraindications. Not priority cue at this moment but important for safety.

opts.text: APPLY FUNDAL PRESSURE during shoulder dystocia maneuvers to help deliver the shoulder + push the baby down + assist the OB maneuvers
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + CONTRAINDICATED. FUNDAL PRESSURE during shoulder dystocia is ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED — WORSENS by impacting the shoulder against the symphysis pubis (the bony obstruction) + INCREASES risk of UTERINE RUPTURE + does NOT help shoulder delivery. Standard practice: AVOID fundal pressure. CORRECT: SUPRAPUBIC PRESSURE (above symphysis) directed downward + inward to displace the anterior shoulder.

opts.text: Apply STRONG TRACTION on the fetal head to deliver the shoulder — exit the head through the perineum with force to relieve the impaction + free the shoulder
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + CAUSES INJURY. EXCESSIVE TRACTION on fetal head is the PRIMARY CAUSE of BRACHIAL PLEXUS INJURY (Erb palsy C5-C6) in shoulder dystocia. Standard practice: AVOID excessive traction + use GENTLE downward traction only + RELY on maneuvers (McRoberts + suprapubic + internal + posterior arm) to resolve dystocia. Avoiding excessive traction = central principle.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What obstetric emergency + IDM + team principles apply?
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying principles.

opts.text: SHOULDER DYSTOCIA = obstetric emergency when normal traction fails to deliver anterior shoulder after fetal head delivery; ~0.6-1.4% all deliveries (HIGHER in macrosomia 9-50% + GDM/IDM 2-4× baseline due to disproportionate shoulder/chest growth); TURTLE SIGN classic; RISK FACTORS = macrosomia + GDM/IDM + prior shoulder dystocia + obesity + post-term + prolonged 2nd stage + instrumental + AMA + precipitous
opts.rat: CORRECT — shoulder dystocia framework + risk factors. Foundation for recognition + preparation.

opts.text: HELPERR MNEMONIC for shoulder dystocia maneuvers per ACOG/AAP — (H) HELP team activated; (E) EVALUATE episiotomy selective; (L) LEGS McROBERTS (sharp hip flexion → flatten lumbar lordosis + rotate symphysis + increase pelvic inlet diameter — resolves 40% alone); (P) PRESSURE SUPRAPUBIC (above symphysis + downward + inward — resolves additional 30% combined); (E) ENTER internal maneuvers (Rubin + Woods corkscrew + reverse Woods — rotation reduces shoulder diameter); (R) REMOVE posterior arm (sweep across chest — >85% success); (R) ROLL Gaskin all-fours position; LAST-RESORT = clavicular fracture + Zavanelli + symphysiotomy + abdominal rescue
opts.rat: CORRECT — HELPERR systematic maneuver sequence. Standard practice. Foundation for shoulder dystocia resolution.

opts.text: AVOID FUNDAL PRESSURE absolutely (contraindicated — worsens impact + can cause uterine rupture); AVOID EXCESSIVE TRACTION on fetal head (brachial plexus injury — Erb palsy C5-C6 most common); APPROPRIATE gentle traction + maneuvers only; document precisely
opts.rat: CORRECT — contraindications + complications prevention. Foundation for safe shoulder dystocia management.

opts.text: LEADERSHIP + DELEGATION in obstetric emergency = team activation within 30 sec + ROLE ASSIGNMENTS in 30-60 sec via closed-loop communication (team leader + scribe + McRoberts × 2 + suprapubic pressure + internal maneuvers + neonatal resuscitation + family liaison) + multidisciplinary + scribe primary + post-event debriefing within 30 min + formal review at 24-48 hr + just culture + EAP + peer support
opts.rat: CORRECT — leadership + delegation framework. Standard obstetric emergency practice. Foundation for coordinated response.

opts.text: NEONATAL OUTCOMES after shoulder dystocia: BRACHIAL PLEXUS INJURY most common (~1-30%; Erb palsy C5-C6 most; majority resolve spontaneously 6-12 months; persistent ~5-10% surgical reconstruction); CLAVICLE/HUMERAL FRACTURE; HIE (delayed delivery → hypoxia); fetal death rare with severe + prolonged
opts.rat: CORRECT — neonatal outcomes framework. Recognition + assessment + management + family education + multidisciplinary. RN role: surveillance + advocacy + coordination.

opts.text: MATERNAL COMPLICATIONS after shoulder dystocia: POSTPARTUM HEMORRHAGE elevated risk (4 Ts of PPH = Tone + Trauma + Tissue + Thrombin); uterotonics ladder (oxytocin first + methylergonovine if no HTN + carboprost if no asthma + misoprostol); bimanual compression + tamponade + IR + hysterectomy + MTP if shock; PERINEAL/VAGINAL LACERATIONS more common; symphyseal separation + femoral neuropathy + bladder injury possible
opts.rat: CORRECT — maternal complications framework. PPH risk + ladder + complications. RN role: vigilant monitoring + medication + escalation.

opts.text: INFANT OF DIABETIC MOTHER (IDM) management: NEONATAL HYPOGLYCEMIA (most immediate; <40 mg/dL; fetal hyperinsulinemia from maternal hyperglycemia → reactive hypoglycemia after cord clamping); SCREEN q30 min × 2 + q1h × 4-6 + q3h × 24 hr; TREAT with early breastfeeding + skin-to-skin + supplemental formula + IV dextrose if symptomatic/persistent; OTHER IDM RISKS = macrosomia + RDS + polycythemia + hyperbilirubinemia + hypocalcemia + hypomagnesemia + cardiomyopathy + congenital heart + neural tube
opts.rat: CORRECT — IDM management framework. Standard practice. RN role: screening + treatment + multidisciplinary + family education.

opts.text: FAMILY-CENTERED + multidisciplinary support throughout obstetric emergency + post-event family meeting + transparent + compassionate explanation; chaplain + social worker + pediatric ortho + neonatology + lactation; cultural humility; emotional support + EAP for staff (second victim phenomenon)
opts.rat: CORRECT — family-centered + multidisciplinary support framework. Standard obstetric care. RN role: facilitate + advocate + emotional support.

opts.text: APPLY FUNDAL PRESSURE during shoulder dystocia maneuvers to help deliver the shoulder + assist OB maneuvers + speed delivery
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + CONTRAINDICATED. Fundal pressure WORSENS shoulder dystocia by impacting shoulder against bone + risks uterine rupture. SUPRAPUBIC pressure (above symphysis) is correct + assists with shoulder displacement.

opts.text: APPLY STRONG TRACTION on fetal head + actively pull head down with force to deliver the shoulder
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + CAUSES INJURY. EXCESSIVE TRACTION is PRIMARY CAUSE of BRACHIAL PLEXUS INJURY in shoulder dystocia. AVOID excessive traction + GENTLE downward + maneuvers (McRoberts + suprapubic + internal + posterior arm).

opts.text: Administer METHYLERGONOVINE (Methergine) 0.2 mg IM as first-line uterotonic for postpartum hemorrhage in this patient who had shoulder dystocia + prolonged labor
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG for this patient. METHYLERGONOVINE CONTRAINDICATED in HTN + preeclampsia + chronic HTN (causes severe hypertension + stroke + MI); this patient has HTN history. STANDARD = oxytocin first-line; CARBOPROST (if no asthma) acceptable; MISOPROSTOL acceptable; AVOID methylergonovine in HTN. Drug-disease interaction recognition critical.

opts.text: Recognize SHOULDER DYSTOCIA + ACTIVATE TEAM within 30 sec + EXECUTE HELPERR MANEUVERS in sequence r/t TURTLE SIGN + failure of external rotation + anterior shoulder NOT delivering; team leader OB + scribe + McRoberts + suprapubic pressure + internal maneuvers (Rubin + remove posterior arm) + neonatal resuscitation ready + AVOID FUNDAL PRESSURE + AVOID excessive traction

opts.text: EXECUTE HELPERR MNEMONIC MANEUVERS in sequence per ACOG/AAP: (H) HELP team; (E) EVALUATE episiotomy selective; (L) LEGS MCROBERTS by 2 nurses; (P) PRESSURE SUPRAPUBIC; (E) ENTER internal maneuvers (Rubin); (R) REMOVE posterior arm; (R) ROLL Gaskin all-fours; LAST-RESORT clavicular fracture + Zavanelli + symphysiotomy + abdominal rescue

opts.text: NEONATAL RESUSCITATION per NRP — WARM + DRY + STIMULATE + assess HR + RR + PPV if needed + chest compressions if HR <60 + EPI 0.1-0.3 mL/kg 1:10,000 IV if HR <60 after PPV + compressions; APGAR; BPI assessment + pediatric ortho consult + PT/OT + immobilization (no traction)

opts.text: DELAY neonatal glucose screening until 6 hours of age — early screening unnecessary + creates anxiety + most newborns have transient mild hypoglycemia that resolves

Delayed screening misses hypoglycemia + risks neurologic injury. Early screening is standard of care.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: FIRST action sequence at shoulder dystocia recognition
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the RN should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: RECOGNIZE shoulder dystocia within 15 sec of head delivery (TURTLE SIGN + failure of external rotation + anterior shoulder NOT delivering) + ACTIVATE OBSTETRIC EMERGENCY TEAM within 30 sec via overhead page + phone + bed alarm + START TIMER + ROLE ASSIGNMENTS in 30-60 sec via closed-loop communication

opts.text: EXECUTE HELPERR MANEUVERS in sequence: (L) McROBERTS (2 nurses sharp hip flexion); (P) SUPRAPUBIC PRESSURE (NOT fundal — contraindicated); (E) INTERNAL MANEUVERS (Rubin); (R) REMOVE posterior arm (sweep across chest + deliver) — RESOLVED at 1547:15 within ~2 min; AVOID fundal pressure + AVOID excessive traction
